summary:
The Aksumite Obelisks (erected c. 1st–4th centuries AD) are among the most remarkable architectural achievements of ancient Africa — massive monolithic granite stelae quarried, transported, and erected at Aksum (in modern Ethiopia) as funerary monuments for Aksumite kings and nobility. The obelisks, some reaching over 24 meters tall and weighing hundreds of tons, were carved to imitate multi-story buildings with false windows and doors, and stood above underground tomb complexes. The largest ever erected (now fallen and broken) would have stood 33 meters — taller than any ancient Egyptian obelisk.

The obelisks represent the engineering and organizational capacity of the Aksumite Kingdom at its height, when Aksum was one of the most powerful states in the world — trading partner of Rome and Persia, controller of the Red Sea trade routes, and (after [[event:2064|the adoption of Christianity]]) a major Christian kingdom. The famous 'Obelisk of Aksum' (24 meters, the largest still standing) was looted by Mussolini's forces in 1937 and taken to Rome, where it stood for decades as a controversial symbol; after extended diplomatic negotiations, it was finally returned to Ethiopia in 2005 and re-erected. The obelisks stand at the heart of the ancient city of Aksum, now a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and remain symbols of Ethiopian national identity and the antiquity of African civilization.
